title: Kundalini and Crying Without Knowing Why: The Body's Release Mechanism

body:
Kundalini and Crying Without Knowing Why: The Body's Release Mechanism

You're here, aren't you? It's probably late. You're probably sitting alone, maybe in the dark, maybe with tears streaming down your face, and you've got no damn idea why. You just feel this overwhelming urge to cry, or maybe you're already doing it, and your brain's screaming, "What the hell is wrong with me?" You're searching for answers, right? Something to explain this bizarre, unsettling experience. I get it. I've been there. I've been you, sitting exactly where you are, feeling exactly what you're feeling.

This isn't some normal sadness. It's different. It's deeper. It feels like it's coming from your bones, from somewhere ancient inside you. And the worst part? There's no obvious trigger. No breakup, no death, no bad news. Just tears, hot and heavy, for no damn reason. You think you're losing your mind. You think you're broken. You're not. I promise you, you're not.

What you're likely experiencing, especially if you've been dabbling in spiritual practices, meditation, energy work, or even just going through a period of intense personal growth, is a form of energetic release. And often, that energy is Kundalini. Before you freak out and think I'm talking about some cult, just hear me out. Kundalini isn't some woo-woo concept cooked up yesterday. It's an ancient understanding of a powerful life force energy within you. And sometimes, when it starts to move, it shakes things loose. Big things. Old things. And those things often come out as tears.

I'm not going to sugarcoat this. It can be terrifying. It can feel like you're unraveling. But here's the truth: you're not unraveling; you're shedding. You're releasing. You're making space for something new. That's what these tears are doing. They're washing away years, decades, maybe even lifetimes of stored emotion, trauma, and suppressed energy. It's your body's way of cleaning house. And it's a messy, uncomfortable process. But it's also incredibly powerful and ultimately, healing.

The Unseen Weight: Why You're Crying

So, why is this happening? You might be thinking, "But I'm happy! I've got a good life! Why the tears?" That's the tricky part, isn't it? It's not about your current circumstances. It's about what's been stored inside you, often without your conscious awareness. Think of your body like a giant hard drive. Every experience, every emotion you've ever had - especially the ones you didn't fully process or express - gets stored. It doesn't just disappear. It gets tucked away, sometimes deep in your cellular memory, in your nervous system, in your muscles, in your organs.

And when Kundalini starts to awaken, or even just when your energy system starts to get a good shake-up, it acts like a powerful defragmenter. It starts to bring all that stored data to the surface. It's not necessarily new pain; it's old pain that's finally getting a chance to be seen, felt, and released. That's why it feels so disorienting. You're crying for things you can't name, because your conscious mind doesn't remember them, but your body does. Your body remembers everything.

This isn't just emotional. It's physical too. You might feel sensations in your body you can't explain - tingling, heat, pressure, vibrations. You might feel a knot in your stomach or chest that suddenly opens up, and then the tears just flow. It's all connected. Your emotional body, your physical body, your energetic body - they're not separate. They're one complex system, and when one part starts to clear, the others follow suit.

I've seen this happen countless times. People who've been through trauma they thought they'd "dealt with" suddenly find themselves weeping uncontrollably for days. People who've always been stoic suddenly can't stop the tears. It's not a sign of weakness; it's a sign of immense strength. It takes courage to feel something so raw and unexplained. Know what I mean? You're not falling apart; you're actually coming together in a more authentic way.

The Kundalini Connection

If you're reading this, you've probably heard the term Kundalini. For me, it was a full-blown crisis, an awakening that felt like an explosion. I wouldn't wish that intensity on anyone without proper support. But even a gentle stirring of this energy can bring about these kinds of releases. Kundalini is often described as a coiled serpent at the base of the spine. When it awakens, it moves upwards, clearing blockages as it goes. Those blockages? They're often old emotions, old beliefs, old patterns. When they get dislodged, they need an exit strategy. Tears are a prime one.

Think of it like this: your energy channels, your nadis, your chakras - they're like pipes. Over the years, they get clogged with gunk - stress, fear, anger, grief. When a powerful force like Kundalini starts moving, it's like a high-pressure flush. All that gunk has to go somewhere, and often, it goes out through your eyes. It's not pretty, it's not comfortable, but it's effective. The book The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) was a lifesaver for me during my own intense period; it helped me understand I wasn't alone or crazy. You might find it helpful too, just for context.

Somatic Release: Your Body Speaks

This isn't just about Kundalini, though it's a powerful catalyst. This is also about somatic release. "Somatic" just means "of the body." Your body holds memory. Your body holds trauma. When you experience something overwhelming, and you can't fight or flee, your body's natural response is to freeze. That energy of fight or flight gets trapped in your tissues, your muscles, your fascia. It doesn't just evaporate. It stays there, creating tension, pain, and sometimes, unexplained emotional outbursts.

Crying without knowing why is a classic sign of somatic release. Your body is finally feeling safe enough to let go of what it's been holding onto. It's like a pressure valve releasing. You might notice other physical sensations too - trembling, shaking, yawning, sighing. These are all natural ways your body discharges stored energy. Don't fight them. Let them happen. Your body knows what it's doing. Trust it. It's trying to heal you.

What To Do When The Tears Won't Stop

Okay, so you understand a bit more about *why* it's happening. But what the hell do you *do* when you're in the thick of it? When you feel like you're drowning in tears? Here's what I learned, often the hard way.

  • Don't Fight It: This is the most crucial piece of advice. Your instinct will be to stop it, to push it down, to rationalize it away. Don't. Fighting it only prolongs the process and creates more tension. Surrender to it. Let it flow. Tell yourself, "It's okay to cry. I'm safe. This is just energy moving."
  • Create a Safe Space: You need to feel safe to let these emotions out. If you're alone, great. If you're not, find a private place. Dim the lights. Get comfortable. Wrap yourself in a blanket. Make it a sanctuary for your release.
  • Stay Present with the Sensations: Instead of getting lost in the "why," focus on the physical sensations. Where do you feel the tears coming from? What does your chest feel like? Your throat? Your stomach? Just observe without judgment. This grounds you and helps the energy move through, rather than getting stuck in your head.
  • Hydrate: You're losing a lot of fluid. Drink water. Electrolytes can help too. Your body is doing a lot of work.
  • Gentle Movement: Sometimes, just sitting still feels impossible. Gentle movement can help. A slow walk, some gentle stretching, or even just swaying can help the energy move through your body.
  • Sound and Breath: Don't try to stifle your cries. Let out whatever sounds want to come - sobs, wails, groans. And focus on your breath. Deep, slow breaths can help regulate your nervous system. Inhale deeply, exhale slowly, letting out a sigh or a sound on the exhale.
  • Ground Yourself: After a big release, you might feel spaced out or disoriented. Grounding is key. Eat something substantial - protein, healthy fats. Put your bare feet on the earth. Connect with nature. Take a warm bath.
  • Journal: You don't have to write about *why* you're crying. Just write about what you're feeling, the sensations, the experience itself. It can help process it and create some distance.
  • Seek Support (Wisely): If you have a trusted friend who understands this kind of thing, reach out. But be selective. Not everyone will get it, and you don't need someone telling you to "cheer up" or "just get over it." A therapist specializing in trauma or somatic work can be incredibly helpful.

The Aftermath: Integration and Care

Once the torrent of tears subsides, you might feel exhausted. Drained. But often, there's also a sense of lightness, a quiet peace you haven't felt in a long time. That's the clearing. That's the space being created. But the work isn't over. This isn't a one-and-done deal. These releases can happen in waves, especially if you're going through a period of intense energetic shifts.

So yeah, how you care for yourself in the aftermath is just as important as how you handle the crying itself. This is about integrating the experience, not just moving past it. Here's what I found helps:

  • Rest: Your body and nervous system have just been through a lot. Prioritize rest. Don't push yourself.
  • Nourish Your Body: Eat wholesome, comforting foods. Avoid stimulants like excessive caffeine or sugar, which can destabilize your nervous system further.
  • Gentle Movement: Continue with gentle movement. Yoga, tai chi, walking in nature. Anything that helps you stay connected to your body without overexertion.
  • Mindfulness and Meditation: Continue or start a gentle mindfulness practice. Not to stop the feelings, but to observe them with more presence and less reactivity. Short, guided meditations focused on grounding or self-compassion can be really good.
  • Bodywork: Massage, acupuncture, craniosacral therapy - these can be incredibly helpful in releasing residual tension and supporting your body's healing process. Even self-massage with a foam roller or tennis ball can make a difference.
  • Somatic Tools: There are some really good tools out there that can help with body-based release and nervous system regulation. Things like an Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set - Lotus Design (paid link) can stimulate points and release tension. A Vibration Therapy Plate - Whole Body Vibration (paid link) can help shake things loose and improve circulation. And for some, a Vagus Nerve Stimulator - Portable TENS Unit (paid link) can offer direct nervous system support, helping to calm and regulate. I've used variations of all of these at different times.
  • Journaling (Again): Reflect on any insights that came up during or after the release. Sometimes, clarity comes after the storm.
  • Be Patient with Yourself: This is a process, not a destination. There will be good days and challenging days. Be kind to yourself through it all.

You're Not Alone, And You're Not Broken

Look, I know this is scary. I know it feels isolating. But you are absolutely not alone in this. So many of us have gone through similar experiences, especially as more people awaken to their own inner energy and start doing deeper healing work. It's a sign that you're alive, that you're feeling, and that your body is intelligently working to bring you into greater balance and wholeness. Does that land?

Don't judge yourself for these tears. Don't try to find a "reason" in your current life situation. Trust that your body knows what it's doing. It's releasing old baggage, making room for more light, more joy, more authentic connection. It's messy, it's uncomfortable, but it's a vital part of growth. You're shedding layers that no longer serve you. You're clearing out the old to make way for the new. That's a brave and beautiful thing you're doing, even if it feels like hell right now.

Keep breathing. Keep feeling. Keep trusting. You'll get through this. And on the other side, you'll be lighter, clearer, and more truly yourself than you've ever been. I know you will. I've seen it. I've lived it.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
